judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports correctly diagnose the owner/FORCE RLS root cause and recommend the same three-part remediation; Report 1 more precisely attributes all three config files (.env.example, docker-compose.yml, infra/main.tf) to the ROTA-214 commit, while Report 2 inaccurately claims the commit's diff was only a one-line docker-compose.yml change, leaving infra/main.tf's role attributed ambiguously.

report_1.evidence: In Postgres, the table owner is exempt from RLS unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is set on the table — and 0003_rls.sql only uses ENABLE, not FORCE. So for the owner connection the policy is silently skipped... docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:2 all set DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:...

report_2.evidence: In Postgres, table owners bypass row-level security unless the table has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, which 0003_rls.sql does not set. So the policy exists but never executes for the runtime connection... the deployment connects as juniper_owner (docker-compose.yml, DATABASE_URL: postgresql://juniper_owner:...)

report_1.evidence: Commit b2a23d2 ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", 2026-06-17)... it switched the runtime DATABASE_URL from juniper_app:apppw to juniper_owner:ownerpw in all three config surfaces: .env.example, docker-compose.yml, and infra/main.tf (confirmed via git show b2a23d2).

report_2.evidence: Its full diff is a one-line change in docker-compose.yml: DATABASE_URL switched from postgresql://juniper_app:apppw@db:5432/juniper to postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper... infra/main.tf also carries the juniper_owner URL as its database_url default, so the misconfiguration is duplicated in infrastructure config.

report_1.evidence: Restore the low-privilege runtime role. Point the API's DATABASE_URL back to juniper_app... Defense in depth: add a new migration with ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_2.evidence: Primary fix — split migration and runtime credentials: in docker-compose.yml (and the infra/main.tf default), point the api service's runtime DATABASE_URL back to juniper_app... Belt: ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; in a new migration

report_1.evidence: (Optionally also add explicit WHERE tenant_id = :tid filters in queries, but the two DB-layer changes address the actual defect.)

report_2.evidence: Suspenders — defense in depth in application code: add WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id to the query in backend/app/routes/appointments.py (the tenant UUID is already available from claims), so isolation doesn't depend on exactly one mechanism.

report_1.evidence: CI/startup guardrails on the connection role: at app boot (or in CI), assert the connected role is not the table owner and has no BYPASSRLS... fail startup otherwise.

report_2.evidence: Boot-time assertion: at startup (e.g. in backend/app/db.py), verify the connected role is not the table owner and has no rolsuper/rolbypassrls, and fail hard if it is.
